verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the barbarous people showed us no little kindness: for they kindled a fire, and received us every one, because of the present rain, and because of the cold.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when Paul had gathered a bundle of sticks, and laid them on the fire, there came a viper out of the heat, and fastened on his hand.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Showed,” he says, “no little kindness to us—barbarians” (as they were)—“having kindled a fire:” else it were of no use that their lives be saved, if the wintry weather must destroy them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then Paul having taken brushwood, laid it on the fire.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how active he is; observe how we nowhere find him doing miracles for the sake of doing them, but only upon emergency.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Both during the storm when there was a cause he prophesied, not for the sake of prophesying, and here again in the first instance he lays on brushwood:—nothing for vain display, but (with a simple view) to their being preserved, and enjoying some warmth.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Then a viper “fastened on his hand.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when the barbarians saw the venomous beast hang on his hand, they said among themselves, No doubt this man is a murderer, whom, though he hath escaped the sea, yet vengeance suffereth not to live.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Well also was this permitted, that they should both see the thing and utter the thought, in order that, when the result ensued, there might be no disbelieving the miracle.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe their good feeling (towards the distressed), in saying this (not aloud, but) among themselves—observe (also) the natural judgment clearly expressed even among barbarians, and how they do not condemn without assigning a reason.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And these also behold, that they may wonder the more.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And he shook off the beast into the fire, and felt no harm.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Howbeit they looked when he should have swollen, or fallen down dead suddenly: but after they had looked a great while, and saw no harm come to him, they changed their minds, and said that he was a god.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They expected him, it says, to fall down dead: and again, having seen that nothing of the kind happened to him, they said, He is a god.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again (viz. as in ch. xiv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 11), another excess on the part of these men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “In the same quarters were possessions of the chief man of the island, whose name was Publius; who received us, and lodged us three days courteously.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And it came to pass, that the father of Publius lay sick of a fever and of a bloody flux: to whom Paul entered in, and prayed, and laid his hands on him, and healed him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Behold again another hospitable man, Publius, who was both rich and of great possessions: he had seen nothing, but purely out of compassion for their misfortune, he received them, and took care of them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that he was worthy to receive kindness: wherefore Paul as a requital for his receiving them, “healed him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So when this was done, others also, which had diseases in the island, came, and were healed: who also honored us with many honors; and when we departed, they laded us with such things as were necessary” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 9, 10), both us and the rest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how when they were quit of the storm, they did not become more negligent, but what a liberal entertainment was given to them for Paul’s sake: and three months were they there, all of them provided with sustenance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See how all this is done for the sake of Paul, to the end that the prisoners should believe, and the soldiers, and the centurion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For if they were very stone, yet from the counsel they heard him giving, and from the prediction they had heard him making, and from the miracles they knew him to have wrought, and from the sustenance they by his means enjoyed, they must have got a very high notion of him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See, when the judgment is right, and not preoccupied by some passion, how immediately it gets right judgings, and gives sound verdicts.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And after three months we departed in a ship of Alexandria, which had wintered in the isle, whose sign was Castor and Pollux.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And landing at Syracuse, we tarried there three days.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And from thence we fetched a compass, and came to Rhegium: and after one day the south wind blew, and we came the next day to Puteoli: where we found brethren, and were desired to tarry with them seven days: and so we went toward Rome.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And from thence, when the brethren heard of us, they came to meet us as far as Appii forum, and the Three Taverns: whom when Paul saw, he thanked God, and took courage.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Already the preaching has reached to Sicily: see how it has run through (even to those lands): at Puteoli also they found some: others also came to meet them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Such was the eagerness of the brethren, it nothing disconcerted them, that Paul was in bonds.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But observe also how Paul himself also was affected after the manner of men.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For it says, “he took courage, when he saw the brethren.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Although he had worked so many miracles, nevertheless even from sight he received an accession (of confidence).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: From this we learn, that he was both comforted after the manner of men, and the contrary.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And when we came to Rome, Paul was suffered to dwell by himself with a soldier that kept him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Leave was given him to dwell by himself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No slight proof this also of his being held in much admiration: it is clear they did not number him among the rest.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And it came to pass, that after three days he called together them that were the chief of the Jews.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: After three days he called the chief of the Jews, that their ears might not be preoccupied.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And what had he in common with them? for they would not (else) have been like to accuse him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nevertheless, it was not for this that he cared; it was for the teaching that he was concerned, and that what he had to say might not offend them.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Recapitulation.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And the barbarians,” etc. (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Jews then, beholding all the many miracles they did, persecuted and harassed (Paul); but the barbarians, who had seen none, merely on the ground of his misfortune, were kind to him.—“No doubt,” say they, “this man is a murderer:” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: They do not simply pronounce their judgment, but say, “No doubt,” (i.e.) as any one may see “and vengeance,” say they, “suffereth him not to live.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Why then, they held also the doctrine of a Providence, and these barbarians were far more philosophic than the philosophers, who allow not the benefit of a Providence to extend to things “below the moon:” whereas (these barbarians) suppose God to be present everywhere, and that although a (guilty) man may escape many (a danger), he will not escape in the end.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And they do not assail him forthwith, but for a time respect him on account of his misfortune: nor do they openly proclaim their surmise, but speak it “among themselves: a murderer;” for the bonds led them to suspect this.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “They showed no small kindness,” and yet (some of them) were prisoners.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let those be ashamed that say, Do not do good to those in prison: let these barbarians shame us; for they knew not who these men were, but simply because they were in misfortune (they were kind): thus much they perceived, that they were human beings, and therefore they considered them to have a claim upon their humanity.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And for a great while,” it says, “they expected that he would die.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But when he shook his hand, and flung off the beast, then they saw and were astonished.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And the miracle did not take place suddenly, but the men went by the length of time, “after they had looked a great while,” so plainly was there no deceit, no haste here (συναρπαγή).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Publius,” it says, “lodged them courteously” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 7): two hundred and seventy-six persons.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Consider how great the gain of his hospitality: not as of necessity, not as unwilling, but as reckoning it a gain he lodged them for three days: thereafter having met with his requital, he naturally honored Paul much more, when the others also received healing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Who also,” it says, “honored us with many honors” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 10): not that he received wages, God forbid; but as it is written, “The workman is worthy of his meat.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And when we departed, they laded us with such things as were necessary.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is plain that having thus received them, they also received the word of the preaching: for it is not to be supposed, that during an entire three months they would have had all this kindness shown them, had these persons not believed strongly, and herein exhibited the fruits (of their conversion): so that from this we may see a strong proof of the great number there was of those that believed.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Even this was enough to establish (Paul’s) credit with those (his fellow-voyagers).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Observe how in all this voyage they nowhere touched at a city, but (were cast) on an island, and passed the entire winter (there, or) sailing—those being herein under training for faith, his fellow-voyagers, I mean. (a) “And after three months we departed in a ship of Alexandria, which had wintered in the isle, whose sign was Castor and Pollux.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Probably this was painted on it: so addicted were they to their idols. (d) “And when the south wind blew, we came the next day to Puteoli: where we found brethren, and were desired to tarry with them seven days: and so we went toward Rome.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 13, 14.) (b) Observe them tarrying a while, and again hasting onwards. (e) “And from thence, when the brethren heard of us, they came to meet us as far as Appii forum, and the Three Taverns” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 15): not fearing the danger. (c) Paul therefore was now so much respected, that he was even permitted to be by himself: for if even before this they used him kindly, much more would they now. (g) “He was suffered,” it says, “to dwell by himself, with a soldier that kept him.” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: That it might not be possible for any plot to be laid against him there either—for there could be no raising of sedition now.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So that in fact they were not keeping Paul in custody, but guarding him, so that nothing unpleasant should happen: for it was not possible now, in so great a city, and with the Emperor there, and with Paul’s appeal, for anything to be done contrary to order.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So surely is it the case, that always through the things which seem to be against us, all things turn out for us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “With the soldier”—for he was Paul’s guard.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “And having called together the chief of the Jews” (v.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 17), he discourses to them, who both depart gainsaying, and are taunted by him, yet they dare not say anything: for it was not permitted them to deal with his matter at their own will.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this is a marvellous thing, that not by the things which seem to be for our security, but by their very opposites, all comes to be for us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And that you may learn this—Pharaoh commanded the infants to be cast into the river.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Unless the infants had been cast forth, Moses would not have been saved, he would not have been brought up in the palace.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When he was safe, he was not in honor; when he was exposed, then he was in honor.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But God did this, to show His riches of resource and contrivance.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Jew threatened him, saying, “Wouldest thou kill me?” (ib.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 14) and this too was of profit to him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was of God’s providence, in order that he should see that vision in the desert, in order that the proper time should be completed, that he should learn philosophy in the desert, and there live in security.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And in all the plottings of the Jews against him the same thing happens: then he becomes more illustrious.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As also in the case of Aaron; they rose up against him, and thereby made him more illustrious (Num. xvi., xvii.): that so his ordination should be unquestionable, that he might be held in admiration for the future also from the plates of brass (τὥν πετάλων τοὕ χαλκοὕ).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Of course you know the history: wherefore I pass over the narration.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And if ye will, let us go over the same examples from the beginning.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Cain slew his brother, but in this he rather benefited him: for hear what Scripture says, “The voice of thy brother’s blood crieth unto Me” (Gen. iv.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 10): and again in another place, “To the blood that speaketh better things than that of Abel.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: He freed him from the uncertainty of the future, he increased his reward: we have all learnt hereby what love God had for him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For what was he injured?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Not a whit, in that he received his end sooner.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For say, what do they gain, who die more slowly?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nothing: for the having good days does not depend on the living many years or few years, but in the using life properly.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: The Three Children were thrown into the furnace, and through this they became more illustrious: Daniel was cast into the pit, and thence was he made more renowned.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: (Dan. iii. and vi.)

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: You see that trials in every case bring forth great good even in this life, much more in the life to come: but as to malice, the case is the same, as if a man having a reed should set himself to fight with the fire: it seems indeed to beat the fire, but it makes it brighter, and only consumes itself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For the malice of the wicked becomes food and an occasion of splendor to virtue: for by God’s turning the unrighteousness to good account, our character shines forth all the more.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Again, when the devil works anything of this kind, he makes those more illustrious that endure.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: How then, you will say, was this not the case with Adam, but, on the contrary, he became more disgraced?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Nay, in this case of all others God turned (the malice of) that (wicked one) to good account: but if (Adam) was the worse for it, it was he that injured himself: for it is the wrongs that are done to us by others that become the means of great good to us, not so the wrongs which are done by ourselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: As indeed, because the fact is that when hurt by others, we grieve, but not so when hurt by ourselves, therefore it is that God shows, that he who suffers unjustly at the hands of another, gets renown, but he who injures himself, receives hurt: that so we may bear the former courageously, but not the latter.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: And besides, the whole thing there was Adam’s own doing.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore didst thou the woman’s bidding?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Wherefore when she counselled thee contrary (to God), didst thou not repel her?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thou wast assuredly thyself the cause.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Else, if the devil was the cause, at this rate all that are tempted ought to perish: but if all do not perish, the cause (of our destruction) rests with ourselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But,” you will say, “all that are tempted ought (at that rate) to succeed.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: No: for the cause is in ourselves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “At that rate it ought to follow that (some) perish without the devil’s having anything to do with it.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yes: and in fact many do perish without the devil’s being concerned in it: for surely the devil does not bring about all (our evil doings); no, much comes also from our own sluggishness by itself alone: and if he too is anywhere concerned as a cause, it is from our offering the occasion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For say, why did the devil prevail in Judas’ case?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: When “Satan entered into him” (John xiii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: 27), you will say.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yes, but hear the cause: it was because “he was a thief, and bare what was put in the bag.” (ib. xii.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It was he that himself gave the devil a wide room for entering into him: so that it is not the devil who puts into us the beginning, it is we that receive and invite him.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But,” you will say, “if there were no devil, the evils would not have become great.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: True, but then our punishment would admit of no plea for mitigation: but as it is, beloved, our punishment is more mild, whereas if we had wrought the evils of ourselves, the chastisements would be intolerable.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For say, if Adam, without any counsel, had committed the sin he did, who would have snatched him out of the dangers?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But he would not have sinned,” you will say?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What right hast thou to say this?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For he who had so little solidity, that was so inert and so ready for folly as to receive such advice as this, much more would he without any counsel have become this (that he did become).

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: What devil incited the brethren of Joseph to envy?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If then we be watchful brethren, the devil becomes to us the cause even of renown.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus, what was Job the worse for his falling into such helplessness of distress?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “Speak not of this instance,” you will say: “(Job was not the worse,) but the weak person is the worse.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Yes, and the weak person is the worse, even if there be no devil.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: “But in a greater degree,” you will say, “when there is the devil’s power working along with him.”

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: True, but he is the less punished, when he has sinned through the devil’s working with him; for the punishments are not the same for all sins.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us not deceive ourselves: the devil is not the cause of our taking harm, if we be watchful: rather what he does, is to awake us out of our sleep; what he does, is to keep us on the alert.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us for a while examine these things: suppose there were no wild beasts, no irregular states of the atmosphere; no sicknesses, no pains, no sorrows, nor anything else of the kind: what would not man have become?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: A hog rather than a man, revelling in gluttony and drunkenness, and troubled by none of those things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But as it is, cares and anxieties are an exercise and discipline of philosophy, a method for the best of training.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For say, let a man be brought up in a palace, having no pain, nor care, nor anxiety, and having neither cause for anger nor failure, but whatever he sets his mind upon, that let him do, in that let him succeed, and have all men obeying him: (see whether) such a man would not become more irrational than any wild beast.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But as it is, our reverses and our afflictions are as it were a whetstone to sharpen us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For this reason the poor are for the most part wiser than the rich, as being driven about and tost by many waves.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Thus a body also, being idle and without motion, is sickly and unsightly: but that which is exercised, and suffers labor and hardships, is more comely and healthy: and this we should find to hold also in the case of the soul.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Iron also, lying unused, is spoilt, but if worked it shines brightly; and in like manner a soul which is kept in motion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Now these reverses are precisely what keeps the soul in motion.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Arts again perish, when the soul is not active: but it is active when it has not everything plain before it: it is made active by adverse things.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If there were no adverse things, there would be nothing to stir it: thus, if everything existed ready-made in beautiful sort, art would not have found wherein to exercise itself.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: So, if all things were level to our understanding, the soul would not find wherein to exert itself: if it had to be carried about everywhere, it would be an unsightly object.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: See you not, that we exhort nurses not to make a practice of carrying children always, that they may not bring them into a habit (of wanting to be carried) and so make them helpless?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: This is why those children which are brought up under the eyes of their parents are weak, in consequence of the indulgence, which by sparing them too much injures their health.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: It is a good thing, even pain in moderation; a good thing, care; a good thing, want; for they make us strong: good also are their opposites: but each of these when in excess destroys us; and the one relaxes, but the other (by overmuch tension) breaks us.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Seest thou not, that Christ also thus trains His own disciples?

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: If they needed these things, much more do we.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: But if we need them, let us not grieve, but even rejoice in our afflictions.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: For these are remedies, answering to our wounds, some of them bitter, others mild; but either of them by itself would be useless.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Let us therefore return thanks to God for all these things: for He does not suffer them to happen at random, but for the benefit of our souls.

verses.book: A Commentary on the Acts of the Apostles
verses.text: Therefore, showing forth our gratitude, let us return Him thanks, let us glorify Him, let us bear up courageously, considering that it is but for a time, and stretching forward our minds to the things future, that we may both lightly bear the things present, and be counted worthy to attain unto the good things to come, through the grace and mercy of His only begotten Son, with Whom to the Father and the Holy Ghost together be glory, might, honor, now and ever, world without end.
